description: Empirisch erhobene Routing-Tabelle für DESi. Jede Zelle ist (task_class, model, k) -> (score, mean_input_tokens, mean_latency_ms, cost_per_item_usd). Quelle: minimaltest-Serie auf LongMemEval-S (25 scorable items), SciFact (30 items), planted-bug code-review (15 items).

memory_recall.description: LongMemEval-S — multi-session conversation history, factual lookup. Tests retrieval + small-context-window pressure.

cells.note: highest score across all 14 measured models

code_audit.description: Planted-bug code review — 15 distinct bug types in 9-module mini-codebase. Tests bug localization with scattered evidence.
code_audit.winning_state_note: retrieval HURTS — 40% recall on generic 'find bugs' question

cells.note: AVOID — worst score across all models on this task
new Pareto-cost king: 0.967 at granite-micro price

scientific_claim.description: SciFact 3-way classification: SUPPORT / CONTRADICT / NEI. Tests retrieval + scientific reasoning with high question specificity.
scientific_claim.winning_state_note: retrieval recall is 100% on this benchmark, k=3 works well

cells.note: specializes in scientific text — strong here

provisional_models._description: Models not yet empirically validated on DESi's three task classes. Three categories: (1) reasoning-mode-incompatible — API integration bug returns empty content; (2) untested top-tier — calibration cost-prohibitive without specific need.

openai/gpt-5-nano.note: Returned empty content on all 145 calibration calls. OpenRouter routes GPT-5 family through reasoning mode where content is empty and the actual answer is in the 'reasoning' field. desi/answerer.py needs to read both fields to support GPT-5.

openai/gpt-5.note: Same reasoning-mode issue as gpt-5-nano. Spent some output tokens (so the request worked), but content field is empty.

anthropic/claude-opus-4.8.note: Full calibration would cost ~\$15-20. Only worth running if a specific need arises (e.g. a task where Sonnet 4.6 is the current ceiling and we need to know whether Opus closes the gap).

openai/gpt-5-pro.note: Also reasoning-mode — both untested AND incompatible with current call_or() output parsing. Two issues to fix before promotion.

hypotheses_resolved.k_star_generalizes_across_providers: PARTIAL — k* exists for all measured frontier models, but values trend higher (k=5-10) than open-source small models (k=3). claude-haiku-4.5 peaks at k=10 (long-context capable cheap tier).
hypotheses_resolved.frontier_models_have_task_specialty: YES — gemini-2.5-pro disappointing (0.28/0.50/0.67); gemini-2.5-flash-lite dominant on code/paper but only 0.60 on memory; sonnet-4.6 not best on paper-audit (0.80, beat by gpt-4o-mini 0.93).
hypotheses_resolved.cross_provider_mix_pareto_dominates: LIKELY — at the Pareto-cost level, the winners per task come from different providers (Granite Micro for cheapest memory, gemini-2.5-flash-lite for cheapest code+paper). Needs mixed-workload re-run to confirm.
hypotheses_resolved.gpt5_nano_is_new_floor: NO — gpt-5-nano returns empty content (reasoning-mode integration bug). Cannot conclude without fixing call_or() to read reasoning field.

balanced.reason: Score 0.60 at \$0.00132 — same score as Granite 8B at similar cost

high_accuracy.reason: Highest measured score 0.68 — 222x cost of cheapest

default.reason: Pareto-cost-optimal at score 0.967 (\$0.00013) — new winner after frontier calibration

high_accuracy_alternative.reason: Also scores 0.967 with proven long-context capability

epistemic_specialties.ibm-granite/granite-4.0-h-micro: Was code audit best-in-class before frontier calibration; remains Pareto-cheapest for memory at score >= 0.5
epistemic_specialties.ibm-granite/granite-4.1-8b: Max score on scientific_claim (0.967); long-context capable (k=10) on memory
epistemic_specialties.meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct: Pareto-cheapest at score >= 0.5 on code and science (\$0.00003); cheap frontier-OSS hybrid pick
epistemic_specialties.qwen/qwen-2.5-7b-instruct: Strong on scientific text (0.90), catastrophic on code (0.37) — task-heterogeneity ratio 2.4x
epistemic_specialties.anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5: Highest measured score on memory_recall (0.68 @k=10) — long-context cheap tier; 0.967 on code-audit; 222x granite-micro cost
epistemic_specialties.anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6: 0.967 on code-audit, 0.640 on memory @k=8; weaker on paper (0.80) than expected; pricey at \$0.07/item on memory
epistemic_specialties.openai/gpt-4o-mini: Solid Frontier-cheap: 0.640/0.933/0.933 across all three tasks; reliable cross-task baseline
epistemic_specialties.google/gemini-2.5-flash-lite: New Pareto-cost king on code-audit and paper-audit (0.967/0.900 at ~\$0.0001/item); long-context (1M tokens)
epistemic_specialties.google/gemini-2.5-flash: Cheap-mid: 0.520/0.933/0.833 — solid all-rounder, fastest latency on paper-audit (0.9s)
epistemic_specialties.google/gemini-2.5-pro: DISAPPOINTING — 0.280/0.500/0.667 at mid-tier pricing; Pareto-dominated by gemini-flash and gpt-4o-mini
epistemic_specialties.meta-llama/llama-3.2-3b-instruct: Fastest latency (0.7s) but weak scores across all tasks
epistemic_specialties.mistralai/ministral-3b-2512: Solid all-rounder but Pareto-dominated by Granite Micro and Llama 8B

open_questions: Does this routing table generalize across more task classes (multi-hop, tool-use, creative)?
What is the k* for Llama / Qwen / Mistral on Code-Audit and Paper-Audit specifically (we only used the Granite-winning state)?
Is there a structural signature (model architecture / training mix) that predicts k* and task specialty?
How does the router behave under task-class uncertainty (when the input doesn't cleanly belong to one bucket)?
Confidence signals: can we read self-uncertainty from logprobs / verdict-confidence and use that for escalation?
